---
title: "Getting Started"
---

## Installation {#sec-getting-started}

### R and RStudio

Download R from [CRAN](https://cran.r-project.org/) and RStudio from [Posit](https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/). This book targets R ≥ 4.3.

### Restoring the Package Environment

This project uses [renv](https://rstudio.github.io/renv/) to pin exact package versions. After cloning the repository:

```r
install.packages("renv")
renv::restore()
```

This installs all packages listed in `renv.lock` at the recorded versions.

### Core Packages

The main packages used throughout this book:

| Package | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `tidyverse` | Data wrangling and visualisation |
| `tidytext` | Tidy-style text mining |
| `quanteda` | Corpus management and text statistics |
| `quanteda.textstats` | Readability, keyness, collocations |
| `quanteda.textplots` | Wordclouds and network plots |
| `topicmodels` | LDA topic modeling |
| `stm` | Structural topic models |
| `textdata` | Sentiment lexicons |
| `ggwordcloud` | Word cloud visualisation |

## Your First Text Object

The code below runs live in your browser. Try modifying the text or the `n` argument.

```{webr}
library(tidytext)
library(dplyr)

# A small example corpus
texts <- tibble(
  doc_id = 1:3,
  text = c(
    "The newspaper covered the election results extensively.",
    "Social media users reacted strongly to the politician's speech.",
    "Journalists questioned the government's media policy."
  )
)

# Tokenise and count
texts |>
  unnest_tokens(word, text) |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE)
```

## The Tidy Text Format

The **tidy text format** [@silge2017text] stores one token per row, making it straightforward to join with other tidy data structures:

```{webr}
library(tidytext)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

texts <- tibble(
  doc_id = 1:3,
  text = c(
    "The newspaper covered the election results extensively.",
    "Social media users reacted strongly to the politician's speech.",
    "Journalists questioned the government's media policy."
  )
)

texts |>
  unnest_tokens(word, text) |>
  anti_join(stop_words) |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_max(n, n = 8) |>
  ggplot(aes(n, reorder(word, n))) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(x = "Count", y = NULL, title = "Top words (stopwords removed)")
```

## The quanteda Workflow

`quanteda` uses a different paradigm centred on **corpus → tokens → document-feature matrix (DFM)**:

```{webr}
library(quanteda)

corp <- corpus(
  c(
    "The newspaper covered the election results extensively.",
    "Social media users reacted strongly to the politician's speech.",
    "Journalists questioned the government's media policy."
  )
)

toks <- tokens(corp, remove_punct = TRUE) |>
  tokens_tolower() |>
  tokens_remove(stopwords("en"))

dfm(toks)
```

Both workflows are used in this book; the choice depends on the task.
